content:
# Color Correction Approach: WaterNet Base + Depth-Aware Grading (2026-04-01)

## Decision
Replace Sea-thru physics-based water removal with WaterNet AI enhancement as the base correction layer. Optionally layer gentle depth-aware color grading on top.

## Why Sea-thru was abandoned
- Sea-thru requires metric depth maps (SfM/stereo). Depth Anything V2 produces relative depth — insufficient for the physics model to produce correct attenuation recovery.
- Output was consistently too dark: backscatter subtraction removes scene brightness, and relative depth can't recover the right amount.
- Green hue persisted despite multiple WB approaches.
- The algorithm targets "full water removal" (in-air look) but the user wants underwater color grading (warm subjects on blue water background, like XHS dive content).
- 5-persona review found compounding color science bugs (B_inf bounds, per-channel normalization, LSAC brightness bias) that explain the dark/desaturated output.

## Why WaterNet
- Pre-trained CNN (1M params) that learned underwater enhancement from UIEB dataset.
- Clean output: no noise amplification, no clipping — the model generates plausible color rather than amplifying absent signal.
- Fast: 3.7s per frame on CPU at 1024px. Would be <1s on GPU.
- Blue water preserved naturally (the model was trained on underwater images, not in-air restoration).
- Simple integration: torch.hub.load, numpy in/out.

## Depth-aware grading layer
- Gentle warm shift on near subjects using Depth Anything V2 depth maps.
- Current implementation introduces artifacts — needs further tuning or may be dropped in favor of WaterNet-only.
- The depth maps are still useful for this graduated correction even though they're relative (not metric).

## What this means for the pipeline
- Phase 5 becomes: WaterNet inference (+ optional depth grading)
- Sea-thru script (`05_sea_thru_correct.py`) to be replaced
- WaterNet model (~4MB) needs to be cached/downloaded
- GPU optional but preferred for batch speed

## Key learning
Physics-based underwater correction (Sea-thru, Beer-Lambert) requires accurate metric depth. Without it, AI-based enhancement produces better visual results with less complexity. The user's aesthetic goal (warm subjects on blue water) is a color grading task, not a physics restoration task.
